description: PCB Defect Knowledge Base for Industrial Inspection

missing_hole.name: Missing Hole
missing_hole.description: A through-hole or via that was not drilled or was incompletely drilled in the PCB substrate. The hole is absent where it should exist according to the design specification.
visual_characteristics: Absence of circular void where via or through-hole should exist
Solid copper pad without central drilling
Visible laminate material without perforation
May show partial drilling marks
causes: Drill bit breakage during drilling operation
CNC drilling machine misalignment or calibration error
Incorrect drilling program or wrong coordinate file
Drill bit wear resulting in incomplete penetration
Stack height issues causing incomplete drill-through
Chip loading in flutes preventing cutting action
Vibration during drilling causing bit deflection

manufacturing_process.process_description: PCB drilling is performed on CNC drilling machines using carbide drill bits. The drill program (.drl file) specifies the exact X,Y coordinates and drill sizes for all holes. Missing holes typically indicate a process failure at this stage.

potential_risks: Open circuit - no electrical connection through the via
Component mounting failure for through-hole components
Assembly rejection at component insertion stage
Signal integrity issues in multi-layer boards
Thermal via function loss leading to heat management problems
missing_hole.severity_rationale: High severity because missing vias break electrical continuity, leading to non-functional circuits. Through-holes for component leads make assembly impossible.
inspection_procedure: Verify against drill drawing or Gerber file
Check drill file completeness
Inspect drill bit status and change records
Review CNC machine maintenance logs
Use AOI (Automated Optical Inspection) cross-reference
Perform electrical continuity test across the via
repair_recommendations: Re-drill the missing hole if board allows rework
Verify drill program and re-run affected panels if systematic
Inspect and replace worn drill bits
Recalibrate CNC drilling machine
Check drill stack configuration and entry/backup materials
Implement drill breakage detection system

mouse_bite.name: Mouse Bite
mouse_bite.description: Small, irregular notches or indentations along the edge of a PCB conductor (trace or pad), resembling the nibbling pattern of a mouse. These are semi-circular or irregular voids in the conductor material.
visual_characteristics: Irregular semi-circular notches on conductor edges
Rough, jagged conductor edge appearance
Clusters of small voids along trace periphery
Reduced conductor cross-section area
causes: Chemical over-etching of copper conductor
Inadequate etch resist adhesion before etching
Contamination on copper surface preventing proper resist adhesion
Etchant concentration imbalance (too aggressive)
Excessive etch time or temperature
Mechanical handling damage to soft copper
Resist film lifting during etching process
Poor resist exposure causing weak edges

manufacturing_process.process_description: During PCB fabrication, copper layers are selectively removed by chemical etching. A photoresist is applied, exposed to UV through a photomask (Gerber pattern), then developed to expose copper for etching. Mouse bites form when the resist does not fully protect the copper edge, allowing lateral chemical attack.
contributing_factors: Conveyorized etching speed and spray pressure

potential_risks: Reduced current-carrying capacity of affected trace
Increased resistance and potential voltage drop
Open circuit failure under thermal stress
Mechanical weakness at the notch stress concentration point
Reliability reduction in high-vibration environments
mouse_bite.severity_rationale: Medium severity because mouse bites reduce conductor cross-section. Minor ones may pass functional testing but fail reliability tests. Severe mouse bites can cause immediate open circuits.
inspection_procedure: Measure remaining conductor width at narrowest point
Compare against IPC minimum conductor width specification
Check etch resist adhesion quality on test coupons
Review etchant concentration and temperature logs
Inspect photoresist application process
Measure etch factor using cross-section analysis
repair_recommendations: Minor mouse bites: apply solder mask or conformal coating if within IPC tolerance
Severe cases: board rejection or rework with copper epoxy on non-critical traces
Process correction: adjust etchant concentration and dwell time
Improve resist adhesion through surface preparation optimization
Calibrate UV exposure unit
Implement SPC (Statistical Process Control) on etch parameters

open_circuit.name: Open Circuit
open_circuit.description: A complete break in an electrical conductor (trace, via, or pad) that creates an unintended interruption in the current path. The circuit is physically severed, preventing any electrical signal or power from flowing through the intended path.
visual_characteristics: Complete break or gap in copper trace
Visible substrate (FR4 or similar) through broken conductor
Sharp or irregular edges at break points
Missing copper section within a trace route
Severed via connection
causes: Over-etching removing entire trace sections
Mechanical damage during handling or assembly
Scratching of copper before lamination
Drill breakout damaging adjacent traces
Thermal stress causing trace cracking (especially at vias)
Incorrect Gerber file data causing missing trace segments
Solder bridging removal that took trace with it
Delamination separating trace from substrate

manufacturing_process.stage: Multiple stages - most commonly Etching or Drilling
manufacturing_process.process_description: Open circuits can originate at multiple manufacturing stages. During etching, excessive chemical attack removes entire conductor sections. During drilling, off-location holes can intersect and sever adjacent traces. During handling, mechanical scratches or breaks can sever thin conductors.
contributing_factors: Design Rule Check (DRC) violations in layout

potential_risks: Complete circuit non-functionality
No power delivery to downstream components
Signal path interruption causing system failure
Potential arcing or spark generation in high-voltage circuits
Hidden failure mode that passes visual but fails electrical test
open_circuit.severity_rationale: Critical severity. Open circuits result in non-functional boards. No component or software workaround can compensate for a physical break in the conductor. 100% electrical test rejection.
inspection_procedure: Automated Optical Inspection (AOI) comparison to Gerber reference
Flying probe electrical test for continuity
In-Circuit Test (ICT) if board is assembled
High-resolution microscopy at suspected break points
Cross-section analysis to identify failure mode
Netlist verification against CAD database
repair_recommendations: Bare board stage: conductive epoxy jumper wire repair if acceptable
Verify all adjacent nets for additional damage
Root cause: review etch process parameters immediately
Check drill file registration accuracy
Implement mandatory post-etch AOI on all panels
Review minimum trace width design rules vs. manufacturing capability
Consider increasing copper weight for thin critical traces
open_circuit.ipc_standard: IPC-6012D - Reject condition for all classes

short.name: Short Circuit
short.description: An unintended low-resistance electrical connection between two conductors that should be electrically isolated. This creates a path for current to flow where none should exist, potentially causing component damage, excessive heating, or board destruction.
visual_characteristics: Copper bridge connecting two separate trace networks
Thin metallic filament between adjacent pads or traces
Copper splash or solder blob bridging conductors
Whisker growth between conductors
Incomplete isolation gap between traces
causes: Insufficient etching leaving copper residue between traces
Copper slivers from drilling redepositing on surface
Tin whisker growth in plated through-holes
Solder bridges during assembly wave or reflow soldering
Conductive contamination (ionic, metallic particles)
Design rule violation - trace spacing too small for process capability
Etchant exhaustion causing underetching
Inadequate conveyorized etcher spray pattern

manufacturing_process.process_description: At the bare board stage, shorts form when chemical etching fails to fully remove copper between conductors. The etchant must penetrate into narrow gaps to achieve complete isolation. As trace pitch decreases (finer features), shorts become increasingly likely if etch chemistry is not precisely controlled. At assembly, solder shorts form during reflow or wave soldering when solder bridges across closely-spaced pads.
contributing_factors: Solder paste volume control in SMT assembly

potential_risks: Immediate component destruction from overcurrent
PCB fire or thermal runaway in power circuits
System-level failure masking root cause
Latent damage to otherwise undamaged components
ESD damage to sensitive components from unexpected current paths
short.severity_rationale: Critical severity. Short circuits can immediately destroy ICs, burn traces, and in power circuits represent a fire hazard. Always results in board rejection without rework.
inspection_procedure: AOI comparison to Gerber netlist
Flying probe isolation resistance measurement
4-wire resistance measurement between adjacent nets
SEM (Scanning Electron Microscopy) for whisker detection
Cross-section to identify short type (copper, solder, contamination)
Ion Chromatography for ionic contamination analysis
repair_recommendations: Bare board: reject - shorts cannot be reliably reworked
Identify whether systematic (process) or random (handling) failure
Review copper weight and etch factor for feature density
Adjust etch chemistry: reduce CuCl2 concentration or increase HCl
Increase conveyor speed or spray pressure for better gap penetration
Verify minimum isolation spacing meets process capability (6-sigma)
Implement post-etch electrical test (E-test) before lamination
short.ipc_standard: IPC-6012D - Reject condition for all classes

spur.name: Spur
spur.description: A small, unintended protrusion or spike of copper extending from the edge of a conductor trace or pad. Spurs are excess copper that should have been removed during etching but was protected by resist overhang or contamination.
visual_characteristics: Small spike or protrusion extending from conductor edge
Pointed or irregular copper extension
May point toward adjacent conductors
Localized at trace edges or pad periphery
Typically smaller than the main conductor width
causes: Photoresist contamination causing localized over-exposure
Dust or particles on photomask protecting small copper areas
Artwork defects in Gerber film
Electrostatic attraction of particles during resist lamination
Etchant shadowing in complex geometries
Partial resist delamination leaving copper bridges

manufacturing_process.process_description: During UV exposure, particles on the photomask or resist surface cast shadows, preventing UV from reaching the resist beneath. These shielded areas fail to cross-link properly, and during development, the unexposed resist remains. During etching, the copper beneath this remaining resist is protected, creating a spur.

potential_risks: Potential short circuit if spur touches adjacent conductor
Reduced isolation distance between conductors
Electric field concentration causing premature insulation breakdown
Tin whisker nucleation point in harsh environments
spur.severity_rationale: Low severity when isolated from adjacent conductors. Spurs become high severity if they reduce isolation spacing below minimum requirements or approach adjacent conductors.
inspection_procedure: Measure distance from spur tip to nearest adjacent conductor
Compare spur geometry to IPC isolation requirements
AOI detection with appropriate tolerance windows
Inspect photomask for contamination under magnification
Review environmental controls logs (particle count, humidity)
repair_recommendations: Isolated spurs: mechanical removal with fine scalpel if accessible
Spurs near isolation limits: apply non-conductive solder mask
Process fix: improve cleanroom protocols for photomask handling
Increase photomask inspection frequency
Implement ionizing air blowers to reduce static in lamination area
Consider automated photomask cleaning system

spurious_copper.name: Spurious Copper
spurious_copper.description: Unintended copper deposits or residues remaining on the PCB surface in areas that should be free of copper (non-conductor regions). Unlike shorts, spurious copper may not immediately bridge two conductors but represents excess copper that violates the design specification.
visual_characteristics: Copper-colored deposits in non-conductor areas
Islands or patches of copper in clearance zones
Irregular copper shapes not matching any design feature
Copper film or haze in areas that should be bare laminate
May appear as discoloration or metallic sheen in cleared areas
causes: Incomplete etching of copper in clearance areas
Copper redeposition from etchant solution onto board surface
Micro-crack in resist allowing copper plating in unwanted areas
Edge plating overspray during through-hole copper plating
Electrochemical copper migration from adjacent plated areas
Contamination of bare laminate with copper particles
Galvanic copper deposition from chemical process bath

manufacturing_process.process_description: Spurious copper forms when either (1) the etching process fails to completely remove copper from clearance areas, leaving thin copper films, or (2) electrochemical processes deposit copper where it shouldn't be. The former is most common and relates to etchant chemistry and dwell time. Thin copper films are particularly problematic as they may be invisible until electrical testing.
contributing_factors: Etchant refresh rate and copper loading
Panel edge effects in spray etching

potential_risks: Progressive oxidation leading to corrosive contamination
Risk of eventual bridging under vibration or thermal cycling
Reduced high-voltage isolation capability
Impedance variation in controlled impedance designs
Antenna effect causing EMI in high-frequency applications
spurious_copper.severity_rationale: Medium severity. Spurious copper does not immediately cause failure but compromises board reliability and electrical isolation. Risk increases with operating voltage and environmental severity.
inspection_procedure: AOI comparison against design artwork
High-voltage isolation test (Hi-Pot testing)
SEM-EDX analysis to confirm copper composition
Etchant chemistry analysis and refresh rate review
Panel sampling at etch process start and end
Measure copper residue thickness using XRF
repair_recommendations: Minor residues: chemical micro-etch to remove copper islands
Confirm re-test after repair passes isolation specs
Process correction: extend etch dwell time or increase temperature
Increase etchant refresh rate to reduce copper loading
Add post-etch rinse and inspect station with lighting optimization
Consider plasma etch treatment for sub-micron residue removal
spurious_copper.ipc_standard: IPC-6012D Class 2/3 - Review vs. isolation requirement

critical.description: Immediate functional failure. Board must be rejected.
critical.action: 100% rejection. Mandatory process hold and investigation.

high.description: High probability of functional failure. Rework evaluation required.
high.action: Engineering review required. Rework or reject decision.

medium.description: Potential reliability concern. May pass initial test but fail in field.

low.description: Cosmetic or minor concern. Unlikely to cause immediate failure.
low.action: Document and monitor. Accept with notation if within IPC limits.

defect_interactions.open_circuit_short: Combinations of open and short defects suggest systematic etching failure
defect_interactions.mouse_bite_spur: Co-occurrence suggests resist adhesion issues in photolithography
defect_interactions.missing_hole_open_circuit: Via-related defects may indicate drilling and plating process failures
